<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678987 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01149: The supported HTML table elements

- page_id: `169678987`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678987/The+supported+HTML+table+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-26T11:42:17.887+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The HTML table element (<table>) is divided into rows (<tr>), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (<td>) or (<th>).

The <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th> elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.

**(i) align**

You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "left" to left-align the content in a cell.
- "center" to center-align the content in a cell.
- “right” to right-align the content in a cell.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

**(ii) valign**

You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "top" to top-align the content in a cell.
- "middle" to center-align the content in a cell.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content in a cell.

The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The HTML table element (&lt;table&gt;) is divided into rows (&lt;tr&gt;), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (&lt;td&gt;) or (&lt;th&gt;).</p><p>The &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt; elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.</p><p><strong>(i) align</strong></p><p>You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p><strong>(ii) valign</strong></p><p>You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content in a cell.</li></ul><p>The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678945 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01150: The Supported HTML Tags

- page_id: `169678945`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678945/The+Supported+HTML+Tags
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-26T11:40:30.308+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### HTML Paragraph Elements

The HTML tags in the Report Wizard’s engine for DocBook defines how a paragraph is arranged in a DocBook document. There are two categories of paragraph elements:

1.1.1 Paragraph 
1.1.2 Preformatted Text

##### Paragraph

The Paragraph element (<p>) defines a paragraph. The <p> element shown in the first figure will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Preformatted Text

The Preformatted text element (<pre>) displays the text exactly as you expected it to. The <pre> tag preserves both spaces and line breaks. The <pre> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Font Styles

Report Wizard specifies a group of HTML tags that you can use to define how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called font style elements.

1. Teletype Text
2. Italics
3. Bold
4. Strikethrough
5. Underline

##### Teletype Text

The HTML Teletype Text element (<tt>) creates teletype or monospaced text. The <tt> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Italics

The HTML Italics element (<i>) renders text slanted to the right. The <i> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Bold

The HTML Bold element (<b>) renders bold text. The <b> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strikethrough

The HTML Strikethrough element (<strike> or <s>) renders text with a horizontal strike or a line through the middle. The <strike> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Underline

The HTML Underline element (<u>) underlines text. The underline <u> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Phrase Elements

There is a group of HTML tags that allows you to specify how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called HTML Phrase Tags or Elements. The following is a list of these font style elements:

1. Emphasis
2. Strong
3. Citation
4. Definition
5. A Fragment of Computer Code
6. Sample Text
7. Keyboard Input
8. Variable

##### Emphasis

The Emphasis element (<em>) emphasizes text content and displays it in italics. The <em> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strong

The Strong element (<strong>) strongly emphasizes text content. It renders the text in bold. The <strong> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Citation

The Citation element (<cite>) presents a citation or a reference to other sources. The <cite> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Definition

The HTML Definition element (<dfn>) gives a definition for the term being defined. The Definition element <dfn> shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### A Fragment of Computer Code

The HTML fragment of code element (<code>) designates part of computer code. The <code> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Sample Text

The HTML Sample Text element (<samp>) designates sample output of programs or scripts. The <samp> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Keyboard Input

The HTML Keyboard Input element (<kbd>) indicates user input or text to be entered by the user. The <kbd> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Variable

The HTML Variable element (<var>) indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. The <var> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Link Element

The HTML Link element (<a>) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The <a> element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.

The <a> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Image Element

The HTML Image element (<img>) defines an image in an HTML page. The <img> element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image. 
The <img> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The underline &lt;u&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook as in second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Underline" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Underline"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for underline.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Underline" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Underline"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for underline.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Phrase Elements</h2><p>There is a group of HTML tags that allows you to specify how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called HTML Phrase Tags or Elements. The following is a list of these font style elements:</p><ol><li>Emphasis</li><li>Strong</li><li>Citation</li><li>Definition</li><li>A Fragment of Computer Code</li><li>Sample Text</li><li>Keyboard Input</li><li>Variable</li></ol><h3>Emphasis</h3><p>The Emphasis element (&lt;em&gt;) emphasizes text content and displays it in italics. The &lt;em&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Emphasis" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Emphasis"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for emphasis.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Emphasis" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Emphasis"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for emphasis.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Strong</h3><p>The Strong element (&lt;strong&gt;) strongly emphasizes text content. It renders the text in bold. The &lt;strong&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Strong" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Strong"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for strong.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Strong" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Strong"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for strong.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Citation</h3><p>The Citation element (&lt;cite&gt;) presents a citation or a reference to other sources. The &lt;cite&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Citation" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Citation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for citation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Citation" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Citation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for citation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Definition</h3><p>The HTML Definition element (&lt;dfn&gt;) gives a definition for the term being defined. The Definition element &lt;dfn&gt; shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Definition" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Definition"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for definition.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Definition" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Definition"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for definition.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>A Fragment of Computer Code</h3><p>The HTML fragment of code element (&lt;code&gt;) designates part of computer code. The &lt;code&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Code" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Code"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of code.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Code" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Code"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of code.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Sample Text</h3><p>The HTML Sample Text element (&lt;samp&gt;) designates sample output of programs or scripts. The &lt;samp&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of sample text.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of sample text.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Keyboard Input</h3><p>The HTML Keyboard Input element (&lt;kbd&gt;) indicates user input or text to be entered by the user. The &lt;kbd&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of keyboard input.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of keyboard input.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Variable</h3><p>The HTML Variable element (&lt;var&gt;) indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. The &lt;var&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Link Element</h2><p>The HTML Link element (&lt;a&gt;) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The &lt;a&gt; element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.</p><p>The &lt;a&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Image Element</h2><p>The HTML Image element (&lt;img&gt;) defines an image in an HTML page. The &lt;img&gt; element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image.<br />The &lt;img&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678990 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01151: The supported style sheet properties

- page_id: `169678990`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678990/The+supported+style+sheet+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-26T11:43:07.819+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports specific style sheets within an HTML element. The supported style sheet properties are as follows:

1. Font
2. Text Align
3. Text Decoration
4. Vertical Align

#### Font

The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.

##### Font Style

The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Font Weight

The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.

The following values are defined:

- normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.
- bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.

The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Align

The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of this property are:

- "left" to left-align the content.
- "center" to center-align the content.
- “right” to right-align the content.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Decoration

The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Vertical Align

The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.

- "top" to top-align the content.
- "middle" to center-align the content.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content.

The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The supported style sheet properties are as follows:</p><ol><li>Font</li><li>Text Align</li><li>Text Decoration</li><li>Vertical Align</li></ol><h2>Font</h2><p>The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.</p><h3>Font Style</h3><p>The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Font Weight</h3><p>The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.</p><p>The following values are defined:</p><ul><li>normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.</li><li>bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.</li></ul><p>The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Align</h2><p>The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of this property are:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p>The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Decoration</h2><p>The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Vertical Align</h2><p>The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content.</li></ul><p>The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>

## PAGE 01152: The Use Case scenario created with version 17.0.1 or earlier

- page_id: `169675545`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675545/The+Use+Case+scenario+created+with+version+17.0.1+or+earlier
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:30:02.523+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case > Use Case Scenario sketch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

880408184

880408186

880408185

Beginning with version 17.0.2 of the modeling tool, Use Case scenarios of projects created with earlier versions are stored in the **Use Case Scenario Obsolete**property group in the Use Case [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- To see the Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group, change the property display mode to Expert .
- The Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group is displayed only if the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case description profile' space='MD2024xR1'] is loaded.

880408183

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679455 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01153: Thrown exception tag value translation

- page_id: `169679455`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679455/Thrown+exception+tag+value+translation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:30:27.964+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Conversion from an Earlier Project Version
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1398533760

1398533762

1398533761

The stereotype «C++Operation» can be applied to an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space='MD185'].

In the modeling tool stereotype, «C++Operation» must apply all operations; thedefaultthrow exceptiontag value is*any*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### C++ThrownExceptions tag value

| Old value | Translation |
| --- | --- |
| Operation does not apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile). | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to any. |
| Operation applies the «C++Operation» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile) and set the C++ThrownExceptions tag value to any. | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to any. Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value. |
| Operation applies the «C++Operation» stereotype in old profile (C++ Profile) and set the C++ThrownExceptions tag value to none. | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to none. Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value. |

1398533758

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conversion from an Earlier Project Version' space='MD2024xR1']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="87d62366-776e-4f00-a43e-426c3c196341"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1398533760</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0d474b4-4820-494e-b80c-85411c752174"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1398533762</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3cc8ce2-9699-441e-8e1e-bae4389f4b77"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1398533761</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The stereotype <span style="font-size: 14.0px;">«C++Operation</span><span style="font-size: 14.0px;">» can be applied to an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD185" ri:content-title="Operation" /></ac:link>.</span></p><p><span>In the modeling tool stereotype, «C++Operation» must apply all operations; the</span><span> default </span><span>throw exception </span><span>tag value is </span><em>any</em><span>.</span></p><p><span><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2018-1-19_14-59-11.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Thrown exception tag value translation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>C++ThrownExceptions tag value<br /></span></h6><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col style="width: 46.893513%;" /><col style="width: 53.106487%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><p><span>Operation </span><span>does not </span><span>apply the </span><span>«C++Operation</span><span>» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile).</span></p></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Operation</span><span>» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the </span><span>throw exception </span><span>tag value to </span><span>any</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span>Operation applies the </span><span>«C++Operation</span><span>» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile) and set the </span><span>C++ThrownExceptions </span><span>tag value to </span><span>any</span><span>. </span></p></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Operation</span><span>» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the </span><span>throw exception </span><span>tag value to </span><span>any</span><span>. </span>Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Operation applies the «C++Operation» stereotype in old profile (C++ Profile) and set the C++ThrownExceptions tag value to none.</td><td colspan="1"><p>Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to none. Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value. </p></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55d27cec-0e38-4458-a5ea-74f49e718667"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1398533758</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="885220a6-f476-4483-be78-2c880b7e0e9c"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Conversion from an Earlier Project Version" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5bf5e5ea-318b-44dc-ace3-c420c52331b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

#### NOTE: Functionalities

Functionalities

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

A Time Diagram is an extension of UML notation. The time diagram is similar to a sequence diagram, but the model elements of the time diagram have predefined stereotypes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Time diagram

## PAGE 01155: Tool interface

- page_id: `169678792`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678792/Tool+interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-26T10:23:58.902+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:

```text

```

All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.

The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.

| Method | Description |
| --- | --- |
| setContext(Context context) | Once the class has been initialized, this method is called by the template engine runtime to set the template context. Overriding the current context will affect the code after this tool. |
| getContext() | Return the template context that is assigned to the current runtime. |
| setProperties(Properties properties) | This method is called by the template engine runtime, once the class has been initialized, to set the template properties. Overriding the current properties will not affect the engine. |
| getProperties() | Return the current template properties. |
| VOID | Represents the Void class. VOID is used to make sure that returning data from the setter method is absolutely nothing. In general, Velocity considers the return of void from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. This causes the word 'null' to be printed out in the report. To avoid this problem, the setter method may use VOID as a return object. |
| class Void | A void class is used as a return in the Tool when you want to be sure that nothing is returned to the context. |
| class RetainedString | A direct command report formatter to keep the referenced String format. The formatter and other reference insertion handlers should maintain Strings directed by this class. |

The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ITool Interface And Related Class.

#### Class Tool

A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.

This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.

#### Concurrent Tool

A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.

The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.

```text

```

An example of Concurrent Tool is *FileTool*. The root template that calls the **file tool** method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the **consume** method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See **TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE** for detail.).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f871750-90ad-43ef-8905-892179d56e4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public interface ITool extends IBean
{
   // attributes
   Void VOID = new Void();
   // inner classes
   public class Void{}
   public class RetainedString implements CharSequence{}
   // methods 
   void setContext(IContext context);
   String getContext();
   void setProperties(Properties properties);
   Properties getProperties();
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.</p><p>The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Method</th><th> Description</th></tr><tr><td>setContext(Context context)</td><td>Once the class has been initialized, <br />this method is called by the template <br />engine runtime to set the template <br />context. Overriding the current context <br />will affect the code after this tool.</td></tr><tr><td>getContext()</td><td>Return the template context that is <br />assigned to the current runtime.</td></tr><tr><td>setProperties(Properties properties)</td><td>This method is called by the template <br />engine runtime, once the class has <br />been initialized, to set the template <br />properties. Overriding the current <br />properties will not affect the engine.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">getProperties()</td><td colspan="1">Return the current template properties.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">VOID</td><td colspan="1">Represents the Void class. VOID is used <br />to make sure that returning data from the <br />setter method is absolutely nothing. In <br />general, Velocity considers the return of void <br />from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. <br />This causes the word 'null' to be printed <br />out in the report. To avoid this problem, <br />the setter method may use VOID as a return <br />object.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class Void</td><td colspan="1"><p>A void class is used as a return in the Tool <br />when you want to be sure that nothing is <br />returned to the context.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class RetainedString</td><td colspan="1">A direct command report formatter <br />to keep the referenced String format. <br />The formatter and other reference insertion <br />handlers should maintain Strings directed by <br />this class.</td></tr></tbody></table><p>The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool. </p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="ITool Interface And Related Class." ac:alt="ITool Interface And Related Class."><ri:attachment ri:filename="Class Diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Tool interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">ITool Interface And Related Class.</h6><h2>Class Tool</h2><p>A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.</p><p>This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.</p><h2>Concurrent Tool</h2><p>A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.</p><p>The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b569d5c-816c-43b7-af13-12a47a93233f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.nomagic.magicreport.engine.ConcurrentTool;
public class LongTaskTool extends ConcurrentTool
{
   public String longTask() {
      // enqueue object
      offer(new ConsumeObject(referent));
   }
   public void consume(ConsumeObject consumeObject) {
      Object referent = consumeObject.get();
      // process long task
   }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>An example of Concurrent Tool is <em>FileTool</em>. The root template that calls the <strong>file tool</strong> method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the <strong>consume</strong> method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See <strong>TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE</strong> for detail.).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673245 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01156: Toolbars

- page_id: `169673245`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673245/Toolbars
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-21T09:31:33.713+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Getting started > Understanding the user interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Restoring default toolbars

INLINE

Toolbars help to speed up your work when performing commonly used tasks. Your modeling tool has two types of toolbars:

- Main toolbars - contain commands for basic project-related tasks and are located at the top of the modeling tool window, right under the main menu.
- Diagram toolbars - contain commands for working with diagram symbols and are located at the top of the diagram pane.

To show or hide different toolbars

- In the main menu, click View , select either Main Toolbars or Diagram Toolbars , and in the displayed menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.
- Right-click the desired toolbar area and in the shortcut menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.

##### Customizing toolbars

You can customize existing toolbars by adding or removing buttons and also create new custom toolbars.

To add a new toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar group and click the Add > New Toolbar .
3. Enter the name of the new toolbar and click OK .

After completing the above steps, a new toolbar is created in the selected toolbar group, and you can choose to show/hide it in the shortcut menu of the respective toolbar area. Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.

To add a new button to a toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the Add > Button . [ATTACHMENT filename='customize_toolbars_dialog.png']
3. In the Add Button dialog, select the desired button or command and click OK .
4. Click OK one more time to close the Customize Toolbars dialog.

A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.

To remove a toolbar or button

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the Remove button.

After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.

#### NOTE: Restoring default toolbars

Restoring default toolbars

To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the **Customize Toolbars** dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the **Reset to Defaults** button.

1421810869

INLINE

Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67428948-3ddd-4460-9978-eac77bf76b73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Restoring default toolbars</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the <strong>Reset to Defaults</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="947a133f-8ff7-4609-a416-b63e2de23099"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1421810869</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /></ac:link></li><li class="_mce_tagged_br"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Main menu" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Image Library tab" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675988 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01157: Tools

- page_id: `169675988`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675988/Tools
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:22:05.155+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['featured']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool provides a number of tools and wizards to help you quickly and easily perform design and analysis tasks. The following links provide detailed instructions for using them:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676294 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01158: Traceability

- page_id: `169676294`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676294/Traceability
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T18:02:45.968+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Model Sample

525815827

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Model Sample

525815831

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Model Sample

525815830

#### INFO: Compatibility

Compatibility

The traceability solution is available in Architect and Enterprise editions.

Our modeling tools support the traceability feature that allows you to track, visualize, navigate, and analyze the elements involved in the traceability relations. The traceability relations are used to relate the elements representing the same concept in different UML models at different levels of abstraction or from different viewpoints. Different levels of abstraction may contain, include, or even correspond to different stages of system development process (starting from requirements analysis and finishing with implementation). The higher level of abstraction (e.g., requirements analysis), contains models with specifying elements, and the lower level of abstraction (e.g., implementation) includes models with realizing ones.

The traceability relations help to determine how your requirements or the other model artifacts are satisfied. As they may change, you can use traceability relations to monitor the impact of these changes.

Multiple types of custom and extended UML relationships (e.g., realization, abstraction), tags (e.g., **Alternative Flow of Events Diagrams** ), and properties (e.g., **Owned Behavior** ) are used to represent traceability relations between the specifying and realizing elements for showing traceability from requirements analysis to implementation and deployment models.

**Relation Map and Dependency Matrix**

You can visualize the traceability relations of your project in order to analyze them using the modeling tool's features, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space='MD2024xR1'] (for the analysis of traces among multiple levels of abstraction) and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024xR1'] (for the analysis of traces between any two levels of abstraction).

**Element’s Specification window, Properties panel, Go To, and Notes**

You may track and navigate the elements, that are directly or indirectly related to a specific element through the traceability properties that will be represented in a special property group of the element’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Properties panel' space='MD2024xR1'], or on the element’s shortcut menu under the **Go To** menu. The traceability properties can also be visualized on a diagram using the standard modeling tool mechanism for displaying property values in notes.

**Traceability Report**

A Traceability Report is particularly useful when there is a need to visualize and verify that requirement analysis, design, and implementation model elements are all covered in higher or lower levels of abstraction, for example, all use cases should be covered with design classes realizing them.

#### INFO: Model Sample

Model Sample

The model used in all the examples in this section is the **Traceability** sample model that comes with our modeling tool. To open this sample do one of the following:

- On the Welcome screen, select Samples > Product Features > Traceability .
- Go to the folder <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\product features and open the Traceability.mdzip file.

525815826

**Related pages**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676351 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01159: Traceability properties in Go To submenu

- page_id: `169676351`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676351/Traceability+properties+in+Go+To+submenu
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-02-26T13:31:06.318+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The submenu of the Go To menu on the element’s shortcut menu allows you to easily find and navigate to therelated elements through the traceability relations.

To select an element from the higher/lower level of abstraction in the Containment tree

1. Select the element in the Containment tree or its shape on the diagram.
2. From the element’s shortcut menu, select Go To > Traceability > Specification/ Realization/ Other , choose a property, and then select an element.
3. The element will be selected in the Containment tree.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability properties in the Go To submenu

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676348 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01160: Traceability properties in notes

- page_id: `169676348`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676348/Traceability+properties+in+notes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:40:30.337+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The traceability properties can be visualized on a diagram using the standard modeling tool mechanism displaying the property values in notes.

To visualize an element’s traceability with other elements in the note on a diagram

1. Create a note for the element, whose traceability properties you want to visualize.
2. From the note’s shortcut menu, select Edit Compartments > Element Properties .
3. In the list on the left-hand side, click the traceability property of you choice (e.g., Realizing Class or Specifying Use Case) and then click the > button.
4. Click OK. Now you can view the element’s traceability with other elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='traceability_properties_in_notes.png'] Traceability properties in the notes on a diagram

The **Traceability** tab in the element’s **Properties** panel (at the bottom of the Browser window) is one of the places, wherein the element’s traceability properties, showing its realizing and/or more specific elements, are represented.

To view an element’s traceability properties in its **Properties** panel

1. Select the element in the Containment tree or its shape on the diagram.
2. In the Model Browser, click on the Properties panel > Traceability tab. Now you can view element’s traceability properties. [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability tab in the Properties panel

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676334 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01162: Traceability properties in Specification window

- page_id: `169676334`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676334/Traceability+properties+in+Specification+window
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-02-26T12:29:01.711+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Traceability property group in element’s Specification window is one of the places, wherein the element’s traceability properties, showing its realizing and/or more specific elements, are represented.

To view an element’s traceability properties in its Specification window

1. Select an element and open the element’s Specification window by right clicking on the element.
2. Click the Traceability property group.
3. Now you can view element’s traceability properties. [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability property group in the Specification window

The following picture gives an example of the traceability relations between the elements from different abstraction levels of the same project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of single level traceability relations

As you see, the “Create User” use case from the “Requirements” package is covered by two design classes, and these classes are accordingly specified by this particular use case.

Therefore, the traceability properties such as **Realizing Class and Realizing Element**represent both design classes as realizing elements of this use case. Accordingly, the traceability properties such as **Specifying Use Case**of each design class represent the “Create User” use case as a more specific element.

Note that the same related element can be represented through different traceability properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Realizing and Specifying traceability properties in Specification window

Accordingly, the traceability properties such as <strong>Specifying Use Case </strong>of each design class represent the “Create User” use case as a more specific element.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: left;">Note that the same related element can be represented through different traceability properties. </p><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column" style="text-align: center;"><div class="layoutArea"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification_of_class_loginDialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Specification window" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><h6><span> Realizing and Specifying traceability properties in Specification window </span></h6></div></div></div></div></div></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676353 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01163: Traceability properties in Traceability Report

- page_id: `169676353`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676353/Traceability+properties+in+Traceability+Report
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-02-26T13:56:21.579+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Traceability Report feature supports the coverage analysis and publishes elements that are related to the selected elements through the traceability relations. You can generate a report either for the whole project or for a selected part of the project. This feature is the output of the coverage analysis.

Coverage analysis provides the visibility of each element’s related artifacts, indicated as realizing (lower level of abstraction) and/ or specifying (higher level of abstraction) ones.

The main objective of the Traceability Report is to visualize and verify that Analysis, Design, and Implementation model elements are all covered.

The Traceability Report provides the ability to:

- Find the areas of uncovered parts.
- According to the report information, create additional artifacts to increase coverage.
- Measure the coverage quantitatively.
- Identify the redundant artifacts.

To generate a traceability report of the selected scope

1. On the Tools menu, click Report Wizard **.**
2. In the Select Template area, select Traceability > Traceability and then click Next .
3. Click Next .
4. In the **Select Element Scope** area, define the scope of the report by using the buttons placed between two lists, and then click Next .
5. In the Output Options area, define the appropriate options.
6. Click **Generate**. Your traceability report will be generated (generation time depends on the selected scope). [IMAGE alt='' src='']A fragment of Traceability Report

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You can generate a report either for the whole project or for a selected part of the project. This feature is the output of the coverage analysis. </span></p><p>Coverage analysis provides the visibility of each element’s related artifacts, indicated as realizing (lower level of abstraction) and/ or specifying (higher level of abstraction) ones.</p><p>The main objective of the Traceability Report is to visualize and verify that Analysis, Design, and Implementation model elements are all covered.</p><p>The Traceability Report provides the ability to:</p><ul><li>Find the areas of uncovered parts.</li><li>According to the report information, create additional artifacts to increase coverage. </li><li>Measure the coverage quantitatively.</li><li>Identify the redundant artifacts. </li></ul><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span> <br /> </span></p><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>To generate a traceability report of the selected scope</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>On the </span> <strong>Tools </strong> <span>menu, click </span> <strong>Report Wizard</strong> <span> <strong>.</strong> </span></li><li><span>In the </span> <strong>Select Template </strong> <span>area, select </span> <strong>Traceability &gt; Traceability </strong> <span>and then click </span> <strong>Next</strong> <span>.</span></li><li><span>Click </span> <strong>Next</strong> <span>.</span></li><li><span>In the </span> <span> <strong>Select Element Scope</strong> </span> <span>area, define the scope of the report by using the buttons placed </span>between two lists, and then click <strong>Next</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Output Options </strong>area, define the appropriate options.</li><li><p>Click <strong>Generate</strong>. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676333 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01164: Traceability relations representation

- page_id: `169676333`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676333/Traceability+relations+representation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:20.950+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tip

1963646019

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tip

1963646021

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tip

1963646020

The traceability relations are represented through the so-called traceability properties that have been predefined for each element type according to the traceability method supported in the modeling tool.

A single traceability property shows an element or a set of elements that are related to a particular element through the relationships which are specified by some traceability rule. The predefined traceability rules are stored in the Traceability customization profile used by the MagicDraw Profile.

The properties for the traceability relations are grouped into the realization, specification, and other groups, owning both direct (single level) and indirect (multiple levels) traceability relations. The indirect traceability relations are represented by the so-called **All**properties, for example, **All Specifying Elements**, **All Realizing Elements**, and **All Specific Classifiers**.

The relations for traceability [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating traceability relations' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability relations representation' space='MD2024xR1'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Navigating between different levels of abstraction' space='MD2024xR1'] is also possible without using traceability properties. However, the customizable model driven traceability properties, which represent the traceability relations in a single place, can greatly help in traceability information visualization and access.

#### INFO: Tip

Tip

You can customize the predefined traceability properties according to your needs or create your own traceability properties and specify your own rules.You can also customize the grouping of the traceability properties according to your needs. For more information, please, refer to the sections [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties' space='MD2024xR1'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Property Groups and Subgroups' space='MD2024xR1'].

Element’s traceability properties are represented in the following places:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Properties panel' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in notes' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Go To submenu' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Traceability Report' space='MD2024xR1']

1963646018

**Related pages**

- Traceability
  - Creating traceability relations
  - Traceability relations representation
  - Navigating between different levels of abstraction
  - Analyzing traceability relations
  - Predefined traceability rules
  - Custom traceability rules

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6ddd5405-d887-4247-97f0-0cdcb78f855e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1963646019</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee48bfff-497a-4a3c-95dd-a578be299ac2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1963646021</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="618a70e5-332c-4cf5-b002-1f9fbe9e1a2d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1963646020</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p>The traceability relations are represented through the so-called traceability properties that have been predefined for each element type according to the traceability method supported in the modeling tool.</p><p>A single traceability property shows an element or a set of elements that are related to a particular element through the relationships which are specified by some traceability rule. The predefined traceability rules are stored in the Traceability customization profile used by the MagicDraw Profile.</p><p>The properties for the traceability relations are grouped into the realization, specification, and other groups, owning both direct (single level) and indirect (multiple levels) traceability relations. The indirect traceability relations are represented by the so-called <strong>All </strong>properties, for example, <strong>All Specifying Elements</strong>, <strong>All Realizing Elements</strong>, and <strong>All Specific Classifiers</strong>.</p><p>The relations for traceability <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating traceability relations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[creation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[visualization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability relations representation" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Navigating between different levels of abstraction" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[navigation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> is also possible without using traceability properties. However, the customizable model driven traceability properties, which represent the traceability relations in a single place, can greatly help in traceability information visualization and access. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ce3d5a0a-5397-485d-bff2-2779fafb5c54"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>You can customize the predefined traceability properties according to your needs or create your own traceability properties and specify your own rules. </span>You can also customize the grouping of the traceability properties according to your needs. For more information, please, refer to the sections <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extending metamodel with derived properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating Property Groups and Subgroups" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating your own property groups and subgroups]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p></div></div></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span> <br /> </span></p><p><span>Element’s traceability properties are represented in the following places:</span></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Properties panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Properties panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in notes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Note on a diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Go To submenu" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Go To submenu]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677109 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01165: Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes

- page_id: `169677109`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677109/Tracking+elements+affected+by+Requirement+changes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-01-13T13:56:10.862+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Suspect Links
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To enable tracking

1. In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Enable Tracking .
2. In the **Enable Tracking**message dialog
  - choose to track Entire Model , or
  - click Specific Scope to choose packages, where the relationships, Requirements, and the design elements you want to track are.
3. Click **OK**. If you're working on a TWCloud project, commit it for the changes to apply.

To change scope

1. Go to Analyze > Suspect Links > Tracking Scope .
2. In the **Select Package** dialog, choose packages, where the relationships, Requirements, and the design elements you want to track are, then click **OK**. If you're working on a TWCloud project, commit it for the changes to apply.

To check for suspicions

1. Modify the linked Requirements as per your needs.
2. If you're working on a TWCloud project, commit it for the changes to apply.
3. In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links , and select Show Suspicions .

The detected suspect links are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To handle suspicions

* marked steps are only available in the TWCloud project.

1. Suspected model elements are highlighted in yellow and marked with a small triangle symbol. [ATTACHMENT filename='suspect_links_marked_yellow.png']
2. To see changes in a Requirement, right-click the validated cell and select **Show Changes***. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Review Requirement changes*. [ATTACHMENT filename='requirement_changes.png']
4. Update design elements according to changes in the Requirement.
5. Clear that suspicion. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Solving untracked links issue

If you notice that some links in your model are not tracked for some reason, you can start tracking them manually.

To start tracking links in your model

1. Right-click the untracked link (it is marked with an exclamation mark in a yellow triangle).
2. Select the Start Tracking solver in the opened menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='nottrackedlink.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
