## PAGE 01228: Using diagram tabs

- page_id: `169674585`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674585/Using+diagram+tabs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-08-09T07:47:39.700+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

568043094

568043096

568043095

To split diagrams in new horizontal or vertical group

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click the diagram tab. The shortcut menu opens.
  - Select the open diagram tab, then drag it to the diagram pane. The shortcut menu opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram_tab_shortcut_on_dgr.png']
2. Select New Horizontal Group or New Vertical Group to split the diagram pane and have more than one diagram opened at the same time.

To split diagrams, more than one diagram should be open.

To turn the diagram window to floating

- From the diagram tab shortcut menu, select Floating .

To show the diagram owner on the diagram tab

1. Select a package and create a diagram in it.
2. Right-click anywhere on the diagram, and from the shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties .
3. Set the Show Owner property to true .

##### Showing diagrams in Full Screen

If you want to see your diagrams in full screen and work exclusively from the diagram, use the **Show Tabs in Full-Screen** functionality. In a full-screen mode, all necessary modeling commands are visible with an option to hide, and the Model Browser is in the auto-hide mode. You can also set the interface components to be displayed or hidden.

To turn on diagram full-screen mode, do one of the following

- Double-click the diagram tab.
- From the diagram shortcut menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- From the View menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- Press F11. You can change the **Show Tabs in Full Screen** shortcut key in the **Environment Options** dialog, **Keyboard** option group.

You can turn off the diagram full-screen mode in the same way as turning it on.

###### Managing Interface Components in Diagram Full Screen Mode

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR1'] is in the auto-hide mode when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR1']tabs are located on the left side of the window. To display the browser, move the mouse pointer over the desired browser window tab (for example, on the Containment tab), and the tab opens.

The main toolbar is hidden when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. To display the main toolbar, set the **Hide Toolbars in the Full Screen Mode** property to false in the **Environment Options** dialog, General pane, and **Display** option group.

The diagram toolbar is displayed when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. Right-click the diagram toolbar to manage it.

568043093

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679636 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01229: Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts

- page_id: `169679636`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679636/Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T13:53:00.366+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin

1. Go to [https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse](https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse) .
2. Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.

To develop a Groovy Script

1. Create a Groovy project.
2. Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click Properties .
3. Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the Libraries tab on the right pane.
4. Click Add External JARs to add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib and in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar .
5. Click OK to close the dialog.
6. Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p>To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to <em><a href="https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse">https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse</a></em>.</li><li>Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li></ol><p>To develop a Groovy Script</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Groovy project.</li><li>Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click <strong>Properties</strong>.</li><li>Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the <strong>Libraries</strong> tab on the right pane.</li><li>Click <strong>Add External JARs</strong> to add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em> and in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar</em>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.</li><li>Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676470 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01230: Using Find operation

- page_id: `169676470`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676470/Using+Find+operation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:21.679+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Useful information

68780004

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Useful information

68780016

68780003

**On this page**

****

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Useful information

68780006

The modeling tool utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space='MD2024xR1'] values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:

- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space='MD2024xR1'] to represent relations between elements.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024xR1'] to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.
- From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart Package' space='MD2024xR1'] to query a model and gather specific elements.
- From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space='MD2024xR1'] to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- From the Specification window of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating executable opaque behaviors' space='MD2024xR1'] to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space='MD2024xR1']**Find** dialog instead.

To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.

##### Specifying a Find operation as criteria

Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).

To specify a Find operation as criteria

1. Open the dialog for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024xR1'] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the open dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Find_criterion.png']
3. Change the **Operation Name**, if needed.
4. Select the **Style** to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the **Relation Criteria** and **Dependency Criteria** dialogs.
5. Specify the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space='MD2024xR1'] , and click OK .

#### TIP: Useful information

Useful information

Selecting the**Search in all texts** check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.

##### expertAdvanced Manipulations

###### Specifying the Find operation properties

To specify the Find operation properties

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024xR1'] .
2. On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.
3. Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property. [ATTACHMENT filename='find_relation_criteria_dialog.png']
4. Click OK . The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property.

###### **find as property**To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024xR1'] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the dialog.
3. In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the **Use as** button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click OK . You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.

68794211

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="231785dc-5bcb-4ad3-bcaa-bc987f91a085"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780004</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ad9c228-6cc2-4c56-b835-de1065ff6026"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780016</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23823620-6e72-473a-a4eb-1649e7e581c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780003</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d9bd883-f80d-4224-8670-309ea8e2cad5" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8e54c0c-9bfa-4a2d-9767-daa76ae79a33"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780006</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The modeling tool utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[derived property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:</p><ul><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Relation Map" /></ac:link> to represent relations between elements.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link> to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.</li><li>From the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Smart Package" /></ac:link> to query a model and gather specific elements.</li><li>From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generic table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Generic Table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>From the Specification window of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating executable opaque behaviors" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[opaque behavior]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.<br /> </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="abe846db-615f-4e59-b8d6-be5f9b6795c5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Find dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Find</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.</p><h3>Specifying a Find operation as criteria</h3><p>Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify a Find operation as criteria</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the dialog for <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the open dialog.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_criterion.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p>Change the <strong>Operation Name</strong>, if needed.</p></li><li><p>Select the <strong>Style</strong> to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the <strong>Relation Criteria</strong> and <strong>Dependency Criteria</strong> dialogs.</p></li><li>Specify the <ac:link ac:anchor="Find dialog options"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Find dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Find operation properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /> </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1e6a977-d642-493c-a2e9-f3cf1e6cdd46"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Useful information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Selecting the<strong> Search in all texts</strong> check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d1a126e-a626-422b-9c1f-995128cfe8a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="">expert</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Advanced Manipulations</h3><h4>Specifying the Find operation properties </h4><p>To specify the Find operation properties </p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.</li><li>Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="find_relation_criteria_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property. </li></ol><h4><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1dee633f-88ad-4f66-bb82-5e8dbb066561"><ac:parameter ac:name="">find as property</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</h4><p>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the dialog.</li><li><p>In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the <strong>Use as</strong> button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_use_as.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li>Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfb704e1-7e83-4c8d-bc46-19c1abd44a38"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68794211</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Specifying+criteria+for+querying+model">Specifying criteria for querying model</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Getting+started+with+specifying+criteria">Getting started with specifying criteria</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+Simple+Navigation">Using Simple Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+Metachain+Navigation">Using Metachain Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+Implied+Relations">Using Implied Relations</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Creating+new+operations">Creating new operations</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Calling+operations+from+the+model">Calling operations from the model</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Creating+script+operations">Creating script operations</a></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676481 space=MD2024xR1 version=3 -->
## PAGE 01231: Using Implied Relations

- page_id: `169676481`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676481/Using+Implied+Relations
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-02-13T13:22:54.040+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

Use the implied relation operation type if you need to specify indirect relations between the elements when going through a definable hierarchy. Implied relation operation collects indirectly associated elements according to the specified **Source**, **Criteria** and **Target** parameters.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The figure above displays composition and inheritance structure between blocks. If a composite block depends on a particular element, the whole block also depends on this element. The same situation exists with inheritance between blocks. Inherited elements indirectly depend on the elements to which the parent element has relations. These indirect relations are referred to as implied relations, and can be displayed in a dependency matrix, as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure, the *High-voltage Battery* block satisfies the *Plug-in charge* requirement. According to the composition and generalization, the *Power SubSystem* and *High-voltage Battery Eco* blocks should satisfy this requirement as well. In the matrix, these relationships are shown as implied, and marked with red, dashed arrows.

Implied relations can be created in:

- Matrices
- Relation maps
- Tables
- Derived properties
- Smart packages

##### Specifying an Implied Relation operation as a criterion

To specify an Implied Relation operation as a criterion

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. In the criteria list on the left side of the dialog, select the Implied Relation operation. The Implied Relation criteria specification panel opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation.png']
3. In the Criteria drop-down list, select the desired relation. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation_.png']
4. In the Direction drop-down list, select the direction of the implied relation:
  - The Source To Target direction means that only the outgoing relations, which are pointing from the source element to the target element, are treated as the result of this criterion.
  - The Target To Source direction means that only the incoming relations, which are pointing from the target element to the source element, are treated as the result of this criterion.
  - If the **Both** direction is chosen, both cases described above are treated as a valid result. A source is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space=''], and a target is the result of the calculation.
5. If you want direct relations to be displayed together with (and the same way as) implied relations, select the Include Direct Relations check box. However, be aware that after selecting this check box, there is no way to visually distinguish direct relations from implied relations.
6. If the implied relation is created in a matrix or relation map, optionally modify the style of the relation by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the **Style** box to open the **Style** dialog, then select preferred style properties and click **OK**.
7. Click **OK**.

Criteria can be other relations than those listed in the **Criteria** drop-down list. Through advanced manipulations, any operation or even a combination of operations can be set as a criterion, e.g., metachain or find operation. By default, an implied relation operation goes through a predefined hierarchy (a composition and generalization) to collect indirectly associated elements, whereas through advanced manipulations, you can define your own hierarchy.

##### Advanced Manipulations

You can change the **Criteria**, **Source,** and **Target** parameters of an implied relation operation. Specifying the **Source** and **Target** parameters lets you define your own hierarchy. You also have more flexibility in setting criteria with the ability to choose from a larger scope of operations, or even combine a few of them.

Implied relation operation goes through the **Source** parameter value to recursively collect elements, and uses the **Criteria** parameter value to navigate from the **Source** parameter results to implied elements. In addition, the **Target** parameter value recursively collects implied elements starting from the results of the **Criteria** parameter. The results of this operation are the results of the **Criteria** and **Target** parameters.

The parameters that can be specified:

- **Source**: specifies the operation that recursively collects elements starting from the Contextual element. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] In the example above, the value of the **Source** parameter is a generalization (direction – **Source To Target**). This means that starting from the *High-voltage Battery Eco*block, all generic classifiers are collected. In this case, the *High-voltage Battery* block is the result of the **Source** parameter. By default, the values of the **Source** parameter are simple navigation operations: a generalization (direction – **Source To Target**) and part (direction - **Source To Target**). Essentially, when a generalization is selected as the value of the **Source** parameter (direction - **Source To Target**), all general classifiers are recursively collected. And, when the value of the **Source** parameter is a part (direction - **Source To Target**), all composed part types (blocks) are recursively collected.
- **Criteria**: specifies the operation that navigates to the elements from the results of the **Source** parameter. In other words, the value of the **Criteria** parameter is the relation for which implied elements are calculated. In the example above, the value of the **Criteria**parameter is a satisfy (direction – **Source To Target**). It means that starting from the result of the **Source** parameter - the *High-voltage Battery*block, the operation navigates through satisfy relation to collect implied elements. In this case, an implied element is the *Plug-In charge* requirement. Note that, by default, the direction of the criteria is **Source To Target**, and that sub-types are also included.
- **Target**: specifies the operation that recursively collects elements starting from the result of the **Criteria** parameter. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
In the example above, the value of the **Target**parameter is a generalization (direction – **Target To Source**). This means that starting from the *AC Unit*block, all composed blocks are collected. In this case, the *Cooler* block is the result of the **Target** parameter. By default, the values of the **Target** parameter are simple navigation operations - a generalization (direction - **Target To Source**) and part (direction - **Target To Source**).

To use an[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='']as input, you can place it under the source of the Implied Relation to ensure correct evaluation.

###### Specifying implied relation parameters

To specify implied relation parameters

1. In the criteria list on the left side of the dialog, expand the implied relation operation, and select the parameter you want to specify.
2. In the open pane for specifying the parameter, select the value you want to use. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation_criteria.png']
3. When you finish specifying required parameters, click **OK**. To set the value of the selected parameter as **null**, click the Reset button.

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Find operation
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674848 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01232: Using layout templates with diagram aspects

- page_id: `169674848`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674848/Using+layout+templates+with+diagram+aspects
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T11:59:55.383+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout templates
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

58117776

58117778

58117777

The Class Diagram that is used as a layout template can have a specific[diagram aspect](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Diagram+aspects)defined. In other words, the layout template can only be used in another diagram with the same aspect. In the example below, the*Control System*Block Definition Diagram is a layout template with the*electrical*aspect defined, so the*Control System*layout template can only be used in the*electrical*view of the system. In this case, the*Control System*layout template is used in the*Climate Hardware*Internal Block Diagram that is defined as electrical. This layout template can't be applied in the diagram with a different aspect defined, e.g., Communication.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Usage of a layout template with aspect. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

58117774

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figure of this page is **Diagram Aspects** which you can find in modeling tool *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\diagram aspects.mdzip.*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc39c676-0229-4fd1-a3bb-2fb1f6478dc9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117776</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c11d2b6c-9800-4e1e-8bbd-5a0a065c1ff2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117778</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6a08214-706a-496f-bba3-f369bd8a6b0d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117777</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The Class Diagram that is used as a layout template can have a specific</span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Diagram+aspects"> diagram aspect</a><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> defined. In other words, the layout template can only be used in another diagram with the same aspect. In the example below, the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> Block Definition Diagram is a layout template with the </span><em>electrical</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> aspect defined, so the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> layout template can only be used in the </span><em>electrical</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> view of the system. In this case, the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> layout template is used in the </span><em>Climate Hardware </em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Internal Block Diagram that is defined as electrical. This layout template can't be applied in the diagram with a different aspect defined, e.g., Communication.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="layout_template_with_aspect.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using layout templates with diagram aspects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Usage of a layout template with aspect. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ec35787-f2ec-4906-9040-76f491c5cfd4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117774</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The sample model used in the figure of this page is <strong>Diagram Aspects</strong> which you can <span class="confluence-link">find in modeling tool <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML\diagram aspects.mdzip.</em></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677229 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01233: Using local project file format

- page_id: `169677229`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677229/Using+local+project+file+format
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:25.598+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Collaboration between disconnected teams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This feature allows you to import *.mdzip* files that have been previously exported back to the server project. Thus, you can simply [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving a local copy of Teamwork Cloud project' space='MD2024xR1'] as a *.mdzip* file and send this file to another contractor. The contractor is then able to update the very same server project from the local project. Once the contractor completes project editing, he or she can send the project back to you. This life cycle may continue between any amount of contractors. The feature works even when multiple contractors work with separate Teamwork Cloud installations as well as local work environments.

The main contribution workflow is as follows:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving a local copy of Teamwork Cloud project' space='MD2024xR1']
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with projects' space='MD2024xR1']
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Updating a server project with local changes' space='MD2024xR1']

To update the server project from the local one, you must have **Administer Resources** [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Permissions' space='TWCloud2024xR1'].

700500

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This feature allows <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">you to import <em>.mdzip</em> files that have been previously exported back to the server project.</span> Thus, you can simply <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Saving a local copy of Teamwork Cloud project" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[save your project locally]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> as a <em>.mdzip</em> file and send this file to another contractor. The contractor is then able to update the very same server project from the local project. Once the contractor completes project editing, he or she can send the project back to you. This life cycle may continue between any amount of contractors. The feature works even when multiple contractors work with separate Teamwork Cloud installations as well as l<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">ocal work environments. </span></p><p>The main contribution workflow is as follows:</p><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Saving a local copy of Teamwork Cloud project" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Contractor A saves the project locally.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with projects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Contractor B gets the locally saved project for editing, modifies it and sends back to Contractor A.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Updating a server project with local changes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Contractor A receives the modified project and updates the server project.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b911a9b2-9fc3-4471-bef7-6f7b7223e7e5"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To update the server project from the local one, you must have </span> <strong>Administer Resources</strong> <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR1" ri:content-title="Permissions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[permission]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: center;"><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc3601cd-5ee1-4932-93ef-fddac2ae9f41"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">700</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XDrcB-JYIHw&amp;t=31s&amp;ab_channel=CATIAMBSE" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">500</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677293 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01234: Using Lock-Free Editing mode

- page_id: `169677293`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677293/Using+Lock-Free+Editing+mode
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:25.823+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Lock-Free Editing mode enables users to work with model elements without the need to lock them. Once the option is enabled in **Project Options**, you can edit elements freely; however, keep it in mind that this is applicable to model elements only. Locks for diagrams, project options, and used projects will persist. In other words, you will still have to lock them before editing.

#### TIP: Recommendation

Recommendation

Assigning differentscopeof the model to different users is the recommended way to work in the Lock-Free Editing mode to prevent conflicts that may occur when users' changes interfere with each other.

To enable the Lock-Free Editing mode in the project, you need to have the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Permissions' space='TWCloud2024xR1']**Release Resource Locks** permission assigned to you.

To start using the Lock-Free Editing mode

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project .
2. In the panel on the left, select General > Collaboration .
3. Under the Locking options, enable Lock-Free Editing , and click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='project_options_collaboration.png']
4. In the **Question** dialog asking whether you want to commit the changes to the server, select **Continue**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] As soon as the Lock-Free Editing mode is enabled, all elementlocks are released.
5. In the Information dialog, click OK . The Lock-Free Editing mode is enabled. [ATTACHMENT filename='information_dialog_enabled.png']

If the Lock-Required mode is enabled in the project, users who worked without locks will not be able to commit their changes to the server. Therefore, we recommend making sure that all the users working on the model do not have uncommitted changes before switching modes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Locks for diagrams, project options, and used projects will persist. In other words, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f9b76e18-7d85-46d1-a229-1196821965cb">you will still have to lock them before editing</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09f91519-1edc-43e4-8d5c-e3d25d948aee"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Recommendation</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Assigning different </span>scope<span> of the model to different users is the recommended way to work in the Lock-Free Editing mode to prevent conflicts that may occur when users' changes interfere with each other.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="951485b2-7ee3-4d01-ae43-57ea26906356"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To enable the Lock-Free Editing mode in the project, you need to have the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR1" ri:content-title="Permissions" /><ac:link-body><strong>Release Resource Locks</strong></ac:link-body></ac:link> permission assigned to you.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f6a5a4d-5969-42c3-b75e-e6fc6a4595d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aMgoxSg4-Rw" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><br /></p><p>To start using the Lock-Free Editing mode</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>In the panel on the left, select <strong>General</strong> &gt; <strong>Collaboration</strong>.</li><li>Under the <strong>Locking</strong> options, enable <strong>Lock-Free Editing</strong>, and click <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="179d8669-f48e-4d99-97b8-2519f62390ff">OK</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong>. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="project_options_collaboration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Lock-Free Editing mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <strong>Question</strong> dialog asking whether you want to commit the changes to the server, select <strong>Continue</strong>. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="question_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Lock-Free Editing mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36b262bb-dce3-48f4-85ae-77e008dd3b84"><ac:rich-text-body><p>As soon as the Lock-Free Editing mode is enabled, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b59000d9-2d22-4021-a891-0cad804e2a32">all element </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b4f21fdc-1f83-458b-9dc2-ebbd76ccd903"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b59000d9-2d22-4021-a891-0cad804e2a32">locks</ac:inline-comment-marker> are released</ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fa12cea8-2006-438c-8591-262f8d2119c6">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>In the <strong>Information</strong> dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. The <strong>Lock-Free Editing</strong> mode is enabled. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="information_dialog_enabled.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Lock-Free Editing mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bab8d01c-9b27-4158-a504-20ea5d28d6f6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the Lock-Required mode is enabled in the project, users who worked without locks will not be able to commit their changes to the server. Therefore, we recommend making sure that all the users working on the model do not have uncommitted changes before switching modes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676453 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01235: Using Metachain Navigation

- page_id: `169676453`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676453/Using+Metachain+Navigation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:21.533+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

2216620902

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

2216620905

INLINE

2145362930

**On this page:**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

2216620904

INLINE

Metachain operation is one of the operation types used to specify criteria for querying models. Use the **Metachain Navigation** operation type when specifying indirect (multi-level) relations between elements through chains of properties.

- Metachains allow you to search for indirectly related elements, although they do not support loops and recursive relations.
- Please note that the Metachain Navigation operation returns a set.

##### Specifying a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria

To specify a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria

1. Click Metachain Navigation on the left side of the dialog box. [ATTACHMENT filename='metachain_navigation.png']
2. Change the name of the operation, if needed.
3. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to set a style that represents the specified metachain navigation symbol. The Style option is only available in Dependency Matrices and Relation Maps.
4. Click the **Insert** button to create a new step of the metachain. A new row is added to the list in which you need to specify a metaclass or a stereotype as well as a property or an operation. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat the previous step to create as many steps of the metachain as you need for specifying the criteria. To remove a step from the metachain, click the**Remove**button.To create another Metachain operation, click **Create operation** > **Metachain Navigation**.

In the table below you can see the options that can be specified for Metachain Navigation.

| Option | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Metaclass or Stereotype | Either a metaclass or a stereotype of a metachain step (see the image below). The first step, when created, already has a metaclass or a stereotype selected by default. This value is the context element type. The default metaclass or stereotype of the first step can be changed. The second step and all further steps are more specific: the values suggested for selection are limited according to the values selected in the first step since you will not be able to add a new step until the current step is not specified. |
| Property | A property or operation of the selected metaclass or stereotype. |
| Unique | Only one identical element will count as the result if the check box is selected.Note: the option is unavailable if Simple Navigation is nested directly under the root union. In such a case, the root union controls the uniqueness.The option is especially important to return non-unique numeric values to correctly execute mathematical operations (such as Sum). |

##### Advanced Manipulations

###### Changing the Contextual element of a Metachain Navigation criterion

To change the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='MD2024xR1'] of a Metachain Navigation criterion

1. On the left side of the dialog box, expand the Metachain Navigation criterion and select the Context parameter.
2. Click the Reset button on the Context specification panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='metachain_navigation_context.png'] The value of the Context parameter becomes null.
3. Select a new context from the list of operations and element types. InformationTo restore the original context, expand the **Other** group, and click **Contextual Variable**.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using the expression of a Metachain Navigation operation as a parameter of another operation

To use the expression of a Metachain Navigation operation as a parameter of another operation

1. On the left side of the dialog box, Click Metachain Navigation .
2. On the criteria specification panel, click the **Use as** button, and select the appropriate command. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Specify other parameters of the new operation.

###### Filtering results by type

To filter results by type

1. Next to the Result Filter by Type line , click the button. Select Element Type dialog opens.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting element types' space='MD2024xR1'] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting element types' space='MD2024xR1'] . 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2216620901

INLINE

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting element types' space='MD2024xR1'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting element types' space='MD2024xR1']

<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2269e333-bc7f-49de-bd1e-fca5880f6eb6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2216620902</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e75692ba-a929-42ee-8200-46ea8faf614e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2216620905</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5fd936f9-81cc-44c2-8b75-d7b32e2f11ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2145362930</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page:</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1ec12e4-9421-4274-81a8-fd3f4410d57a" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5999ec96-5d6e-414f-978a-956d491b4ba8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2216620904</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d65f56db-8ba0-442a-80b4-31f98fe66cf9">Metachain operation is one of the operation types used to specify criteria for querying models. U</ac:inline-comment-marker>se the <strong>Metachain Navigation</strong> operation type when specifying indirect (multi-level) relations between elements through chains of properties.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1c5b055-75da-4890-a67f-824f01a50bcb"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span>Metachains allow you to search for indirectly related elements, although they do not support loops and recursive relations.</span></li><li><span><span>Please note that the Metachain Navigation operation returns a set.</span><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Specifying a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria</h3><p>To specify a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Metachain Navigation</strong> on the left side of the dialog box.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="metachain_navigation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Metachain Navigation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Change the name of the operation, if needed.</li><li><p>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Metachain Navigation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to set a style that represents the specified metachain navigation symbol.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3a1049c-627e-45ba-91e7-b9976f423814"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Style option is only available in Dependency Matrices and Relation Maps.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Insert</strong> button to create a new step of the metachain. A new row is added to the list in which you need to specify a metaclass or a stereotype as well as a property or an operation.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="metaclass_stereotype_property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Metachain Navigation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li><p>Repeat the previous step to create as many steps of the metachain as you need for specifying the criteria.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6ac1a8a5-0c8a-438b-a4f5-f8aaea779807"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>To remove a step from the metachain, click the </span><strong>Remove</strong><span> button</span><span>.<br /></span>To create another Metachain operation, click <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Create operation</strong> &gt; <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Metachain Navigation</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p>In the table below you can see the options that can be specified for Metachain Navigation.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 100.0%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 9.59082%;" /><col style="width: 78.8314%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Option</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><p>Metaclass <br />or <br />Stereotype</p></td><td><p>Either a metaclass or a stereotype of a metachain step (see the image below). The first step, when created, already has a metaclass or a stereotype selected by default. This value is the context element type. The default metaclass or stereotype of the first step can be changed. The second step and all further steps are more specific: the values suggested for selection are limited according to the values selected in the first step since you will not be able to add a new step until the current step is not specified.</p></td></tr><tr><td>Property</td><td>A property or operation of the selected metaclass or stereotype.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><span>Unique</span></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Only one identical element will count as the result if the check box is selected.</span></p><p><strong>Note</strong>: the option is unavailable if Simple Navigation is nested directly under the root union. In such a case, the root union controls the uniqueness.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d5690b9-e0f3-4bda-99ce-0a3349591cce"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The option is especially important to return non-unique numeric values to correctly execute mathematical operations (such as Sum).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Advanced Manipulations</h3><h4>Changing the Contextual element of a Metachain Navigation criterion</h4><p>To change the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Contextual element]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of a Metachain Navigation criterion</p><hr /><ol><li>On the left side of the dialog box, expand the <strong>Metachain Navigation</strong> criterion and select the <strong>Context</strong> parameter.</li><li>Click the <strong>Reset</strong> button on the <strong>Context</strong> specification panel.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="metachain_navigation_context.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Metachain Navigation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The value of the <strong>Context</strong> parameter becomes null. </li><li><p>Select a new context from the list of operations and element types.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="12054701-0cd0-4667-99aa-5cedbfb99c93"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To restore the original context, expand the <strong>Other</strong> group, and click <strong>Contextual Variable</strong>.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="contextual_variable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676264 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01236: Using Model Patch

- page_id: `169676264`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676264/Using+Model+Patch
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-12-17T12:14:31.456+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Patch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**NotePlease note before creating a model patch!**

Since model elements are usually related to and depend on other elements, you must include the necessary elements in the scope. The Model Patch mechanism does not automatically check dependencies or include them in the patch data.

To avoid data loss:

- Go to **Related Elements > Depends On** and check if you are bringing all the elements that your elements depend on; or
- Ensure that the target version (the project where you are applying the patch) already has the dependent elements.

For example, you have Part A that has Type B. If you want to bring Part A, you must either:

- Include Type B in the patch as well, or
- Make sure Type B already exists in the target.

Failure to do this will lead to data loss, as Part A will be created without a type.

##### Creating a model patch

To create a model patch

1. Open the source project.
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select Tools > Patch > Create Patch .
  - Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select Tools > Create Patch.
3. Select the elements you want to include in the patch using the Add , Remove , Remove , and All buttons. If you select an element that has child elements, all child elements are automatically included in the patch. [ATTACHMENT filename='selecting_patch_scope.png']
4. Click Next .
5. In the next step, select the type of patch you want to create:
  - All selected content - This type of patch brings all the selected scope to the target project potentially overriding existing elements.
  - Changes from specific version. This type of patch adds the selected scope to the target project as concurrent changes. If you select this patch type, also select a project version. All changes from the selected version, subsequent versions, and the current project version will be included in the patch. This patch type is only available for Teamwork Cloud projects. [ATTACHMENT filename='patch_types.png']
6. Click Next.
7. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three_dots_button.png'] and select the location where you want to save the patch file.
8. Click Save.

##### Applying model patch

To apply a model patch

1. Open the target project.
2. In the main menu, select Tools > Patch > Apply Patch .
3. Select the patch file and click Open.
4. Investigate the results:
  - If you receive a message that the patch is applied successfully, it means all changes from the patch were successfully applied.
  - If you receive a warning that the patch has been applied, but some changes could not be applied, click **OK** and review the changes that were not applied in the open **Changes That Are Not Applied** pane. In the **Changes That Are Not Applied** pane, select each change and read the description explaining the reason of the issue. To avoid such issues, remember tobefore creating a model patch. [ATTACHMENT filename='changes_that_are_not_applied.png']

To avoid such issues, remember to </span><ac:link ac:anchor="Note"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[check element dependencies ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">before creating a model patch.</span></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="changes_that_are_not_applied.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679635 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01237: Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts

- page_id: `169679635`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679635/Using+NetBeans+IDE+to+develop+Groovy+Scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T13:50:56.014+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You will need NetBeans IDE 6.7.1 or 6.8 with Groovy Plugin to develop Groovy scripts. The Groovy support comes with NetBeans "Java" and "All" package. If your NetBeans does not have Groovy, download the plugin through NetBeans Plugin Manager. For more information about adding a new plugin, click**IDE Basics** >**Plugins** > **Updating the IDE** on the NetBeans **Help** menu.

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Set up a classpath.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To set up a classpath

1. Click Tools > Libraries on the main menu. The Library Manager dialog will open.
2. Click the New Library button. The New Library dialog will open.
3. Specify a library name, for example, MD16.8. The Library Type must be Class Libraries.
4. Click OK to close the New Library dialog.
5. Select your new library in the Libraries tree.
6. Click the Add JAR/Folder button and add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib .
7. Repeat steps 2 to 6 to add the Groovy library that is in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar.
8. Click OK to close the Library Manager dialog.

To develop a Groovy script

1. Click File > New Project on the main menu to create a Java application project. The New Project dialog will open.
2. Select Java in the Categories box and Java Application in the Projects box, and then click Next .
3. Choose a project location. Be sure that you do not select Create Main Class .
4. Click Finish .
5. Expand your project node in the Project window. The Libraries node will appear.
6. Right-click the Libraries node and select Add Library from the shortcut menu.
7. Click File > New File on the main menu to add a new Groovy file.
8. Select Groovy in the Categories box and Groovy Script in the File Types box.
9. Follow the instructions until finish.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

For more information about adding a new plugin, click<strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"> IDE Basics</strong> &gt;<strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"> Plugins</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Updating the IDE</strong> on the NetBeans <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Help</strong> menu.</p><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Set up a classpath.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To set up a classpath</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Libraries</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Click the <strong>New Library</strong> button. The <strong>New Library</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Specify a library name, for example, MD16.8. The Library Type must be Class Libraries.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>New Library</strong> dialog.</li><li>Select your new library in the Libraries tree.</li><li>Click the <strong>Add JAR/Folder</strong> button and add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em>.</li><li>Repeat steps 2 to 6 to add the Groovy library that is in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar.</em></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To develop a Groovy script</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu to create a Java application project. The <strong>New Project</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong>Java</strong> in the <strong>Categories</strong> box and <strong>Java Application</strong> in the<strong> Projects</strong> box, and then click <strong>Next</strong>.</li><li>Choose a project location. Be sure that you do not select <strong>Create Main Class</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Finish</strong>.</li><li>Expand your project node in the <strong>Project</strong> window. The Libraries node will appear.</li><li>Right-click the Libraries node and select <strong>Add Library</strong> from the shortcut menu.</li><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New File</strong> on the main menu to add a new Groovy file.</li><li>Select Groovy in the <strong>Categories</strong> box and Groovy Script in the <strong>File Types</strong> box.</li><li>Follow the instructions until finish.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673676 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01238: Using other projects in a project

- page_id: `169673676`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673676/Using+other+projects+in+a+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-03-20T14:28:54.131+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Project partitioning > Composing model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

305408088

305408090

305408089

When a project is used in another project, shared elements of the used project become visible and accessiblein the Containment tree of the project that uses them as if they were a part of the project.

##### Using local projects

To start using a project

1. Do one of the following: The Use Project wizard opens.
  - From the File menu, select Use Project > Use Local Project .
  - From the Options menu select Project Usages and click the Use Project button.
2. In the Select project step, select the project you want to use from the file system or from the predefined location:
  - If you want to use your custom project from a custom location, select to use a project From file system and click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_button.png'] next to the Project file box to navigate to the desired file.
  - If you want to use the project coming with modeling tool installation, select to use a project**From predefined**location, then select a path from the**Paths to used projects**list and the project to use fromthe list below. You can customize the **Paths to used projects** list and add your own paths by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating path variables' space='MD2024xR1'].
3. Click the Next button. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the Specify usage options step, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing project usages' space='MD2024xR1'] , such as the accessibility mode, load mode, and mounting locations of shared packages.
5. Click the Finish button.

After your project starts using another project, the elements of the used project are still stored separately:

- Used project elements — in the used project file.
- Project elements — in the project file.

##### Modifying project accessibility

The accessibility mode of the used project can be specified in the **Used Projects** dialog. By default, projects are used in the read-only mode.

To change the accessibility mode of a used project

1. From the Options menu, select Project Usages . The Used Projects dialog opens.
2. In the tree on the left side of the open dialog, select the used project and click the Read-only or Read-write option in the Accessibility area.
3. Click OK .

If you need to change the contents of the project used in the read-only mode, you can open it separately from the project that uses it. For this, click the **Open Project** button in the same dialog.

305408087

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing models' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing used projects' space='MD2024xR1']

click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using other projects in a project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><strong> </strong>next to the <strong>Project file</strong> box to navigate to the desired file.</li><li><p><span>If you want to use the project coming with modeling tool installation, select to use a project </span><strong>From predefined</strong><span> location, then select a path from the </span><strong>Paths to used projects</strong><span> list and the project to use from </span>the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="02744562-58ca-481a-974f-e322b623e4b5">list below</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c59cfd48-a1b2-457f-a71a-7762dafb3718"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can customize the <strong>Paths to used projects</strong> list and add your own paths by <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating path variables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[creating path variables]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li>Click the <strong>Next</strong> button.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image ac:title="Selecting to use project from predefined location" ac:alt="Selecting to use project from predefined location"><ri:attachment ri:filename="use_project_wizard.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using other projects in a project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span><br /><br /></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="20aab0f4-ef69-4e20-945e-83bbd584d028">In the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="20aab0f4-ef69-4e20-945e-83bbd584d028">Specify usage options</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="20aab0f4-ef69-4e20-945e-83bbd584d028"> step,</ac:inline-comment-marker> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="24fd8a16-42ad-4589-aafb-1bce849bfa28"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Managing project usages" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specify the project usage options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></ac:inline-comment-marker>, such as <span>the accessibility mode, load mode, and mounting locations <span>of shared packages.</span></span></li><li>Click the <strong>Finish</strong> button.</li></ol><p>After your project starts using another project, the elements of the used project are still stored separately:</p><ul><li>Used project elements — in the used project file.</li><li>Project elements — in the project file.</li></ul><h3>Modifying project accessibility</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="abd1ad40-ec60-46e8-9e09-a5f70ae64aed">The acces</ac:inline-comment-marker>sibility mode of the used project can be specified in the <strong>Used Projects</strong> dialog. By default, projects are used in the read-only mode.</p><p>To change the accessibility mode of a used project</p><hr /><ol><li>From the <strong>Options</strong> menu, select <strong>Project Usages</strong>. The <strong>Used Projects</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the tree on the left side of the open dialog, select the used project and click the <strong>Read-only</strong> or <strong>Read-write</strong> option in the <strong>Accessibility</strong> area.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p>If you need to change the contents of the project used in the read-only mode, you can open it separately from the project that uses it. For this, click the <strong>Open Project</strong> button in the same dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e8fa884-63cc-41db-925a-73ab8bee6e1f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305408087</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Importing models" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Managing used projects" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676494 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01239: Using Property Test operation

- page_id: `169676494`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676494/Using+Property+Test+operation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:22.093+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Property information

69029293

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Property information

69029305

69029292

**On this page**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Property information

69029294

A Property Test operation checks if model elements contain the selected properties with the specified values. If the property values of a model element match the specified property values, the operation returns *true*, and if they do not match -*false.*

##### Specifying a Property Test operation

To specify a Property Test operation

1. Open a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024xR1'] .
2. From the operation list on the left side of the dialog, select the**Property Test**operation. If Property Test operation node is not displayed, select **Create operation**, and then select **Property Test** in the **Operations** area on the right side of the dialog. The **Property Test** panel appears.
3. From the property list, select the desired properties and specify their values, as shown below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] ImportantIf**Show test criteria available only for context**is set to true, the list of properties available for testing is reduced to properties applicable only to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='MD2024xR1'] type.
4. Click OK and close the dialog for querying the model if it is still open.

After specifying a Property Test operation, it checks if model elements contain the property values that you specified in the step 3. For example, if you select the **risk** property and set it to **High**, as highlighted in the figure above, the Property Test operation returns *true* for all model elements with the **risk** property set to **High**. Advanced operation properties can be specified through advanced manipulations.

##### Advanced Manipulations

###### Specifying a Property Test operation through advanced manipulations

To specify a Property Test operation through advanced manipulations

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024xR1'] .
2. In the operation list on the left side of the dialog, expand the Property Test node.
3. Select the desired property from the list under the Property Test node highlighted in the figure below, then specify its value in the specification panel on the right side of the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='specifying_property_test_properties.png']
4. Click OK and close the dialog for querying model if it is still open.

#### NOTE: Property information

Property information

If **AND Operator** is set to *true* (default value), the operation returns *true* only for the elements that contain all of the selected properties with specified values. If **AND Operator** is set to *false*, the operation returns *true* for the elements that contain at least one of the selected properties with the specified value.

69053065

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Find operation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Using Property Test operation
  - Creating new operations

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677210 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01240: Using server project file format

- page_id: `169677210`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677210/Using+server+project+file+format
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:22:07.677+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Collaboration between disconnected teams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To ensure faster project export and import while retaining the same project structure, you can use the file exchange format *.szip*. Using this format, you can export a project with or without used projects, add a project as a new server project, and update it from a locally saved server project file.

You cannot open server project files locally. To view the contents of a project, you must add the project or update it using the *.szip* file.

**In this section:**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p style="margin-left: 0.0px;"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To ensure faster project export and import while retaining the same project structure, you can use the file exchange format <em>.szip</em>. Using this format, you can export a project with or without used projects, add a project as a new server project, and update it from a locally saved server project file<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e2806afd-db43-4169-8303-abfd7054376c">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bbff92c-4d1e-40a0-9efe-709b8046ce9f"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You cannot open server project files locally. To view the contents of a project, you must add the project or update it using the <em>.szip</em> file.</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 0.0px;"><strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In this section:</span></strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="3feaa75c-bd9e-42e9-81c5-a21dff605936" /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
